title: Paget Disease of Bone
oneLiner: A chronic metabolic bone disorder causing focal, disorganised bone remodelling; common in elderly UK/Irish population, leading to pain, deformity, fracture, and rarely high-output failure.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Pathological fracture** (especially femoral neck or shaft) – acute, severe pain, inability to weight bear, limb deformity; this is a surgical emergency, orthogeriatric requirement.
- **Spinal cord compression** from pagetic vertebral expansion or fracture – new onset back pain, leg weakness, urinary retention, sensory level; needs urgent MRI and neurosurgery consult.
- **High-output cardiac failure** (rare but lethal) – widespread polyostotic disease with warm, vascular bone; look for tachycardia, wide pulse pressure, signs of heart failure in an elderly patient with known Paget’s.
sections.Presentation:
- Bone pain (deep, aching, worse at night, improved by lying down) – typically pelvis, femur, skull, spine.
- Deformity: bowing of tibia or femur, skull enlargement, “hat size increase”.
- Warmth over affected bone (increased vascularity).
- Hearing loss (bilateral, conductive ± sensorineural due to skull base involvement).
- Neurological: spinal stenosis, cranial nerve palsies (e.g., optic atrophy), basilar invagination.
- Incidental finding on X-ray (asymptomatic) – very common.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Osteoarthritis** – pain worse with activity, joint-line tenderness, no bone deformity or warmth; X-ray shows joint space narrowing, osteophytes, not pagetic trabeculae.
- **Metastatic bone disease (prostate, breast, lung, renal)** – often lytic or blastic lesions, may have history of malignancy; check PSA, alkaline phosphatase may be raised but also raised in Paget’s; bone scan can help but appearance differs.
- **Osteomalacia** – widespread bone pain, muscle weakness, low serum calcium/phosphate, raised ALP; imaging shows Looser zones (pseudofractures), not the coarse trabeculae of Paget’s.
- **Hyperparathyroidism (osteitis fibrosa cystica)** – hypercalcaemia, raised PTH, subperiosteal bone resorption on X-ray; Paget’s has normal calcium and PTH.
- **Fibrous dysplasia** – presents earlier (children/young adults), often monostotic, X-ray shows “ground glass” matrix; not a disease of the elderly.
- **Splitter:** If ALP >500 U/L and X-ray shows thickened, coarsened trabeculae with bone enlargement → Paget’s; if ALP high but calcium high and X-ray shows subperiosteal resorption → hyperparathyroidism.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Measure height (serial loss may indicate vertebral fracture), check pulses and capillary refill in affected limbs; do a basic neurological exam (tone, power, sensation, reflexes, plantar responses).
- **Bloods:**
  - ALP (bone-specific isoenzyme if available) – main marker; very high in active disease (often >500 U/L, can be >2000).
  - Calcium, phosphate – normal.
  - PTH – normal (may be secondarily raised if calcium low – rare).
  - 25-hydroxyvitamin D – check before bisphosphonate therapy.
  - Urea and creatinine (for renal function before IV bisphosphonate).
  - FBC, ESR/CRP (to exclude other causes of raised ALP, e.g., liver disease).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Plain X-ray of symptomatic area – diagnostic features: osteoporosis circumscripta cranii (skull), coarsened trabeculae, bone enlargement, “picture frame” vertebra, “bowed” long bones, fissure fractures.
  - **Second-line:** Technetium-99m bone scan – to assess extent of polyostotic disease (bilateral, asymmetrical uptake). Can be used to guide therapy if localised.
  - MRI if spinal cord compression or suspected pathological fracture.
  - CT if planning orthopaedic surgery.
- **Never-miss test:** Plain X-ray of any painful bone (to rule out fracture) + ALP level – omission may mean treating pain as arthritis while missing incipient pathological fracture.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute fracture or major deformity: immobilise, analgesia, orthogeriatric referral; do not delay surgery.
  - If suspected cord compression: urgent MRI, dexamethasone (10 mg IV stat), consult neurosurgery.
  - If high-output failure: treat heart failure (diuretics, rate control) and urgent Paget’s therapy.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis with X-ray and labs.
  - Assess pain: simple analgesia (paracetamol, NSAIDs if not contraindicated) – but do not delay definitive therapy.
  - Start bisphosphonate therapy (see Drugs & Doses) – NICE recommends treatment for symptomatic disease or to prevent complications (e.g., at risk sites, deformity, deafness).
  - Ensure good vitamin D and calcium intake (oral calcium 500-1000 mg daily + vitamin D 800-2000 IU daily) – crucial before/during bisphosphonate to avoid hypocalcaemia.
  - Physiotherapy review for gait aids, exercises.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Monitor ALP every 6-12 months; aim to normalise or reduce by ≥50%.
  - Repeat bisphosphonate if ALP rises again (usually 1-2 years later).
  - Annual hearing assessment if skull involvement.
  - Orthopaedic follow-up if deformity or joint involvement.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reason):**
  - Fluids: 1 L Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline over 8-12h (if nil otherwise) – no special need unless pre-bisphosphonate loading.
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD if weight >50 kg, renal function ok).
  - Diet/NBM: unless fracture management, allow normal diet.
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly; if high-output failure or acute fracture, 1-2 hourly initially.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - **Zoledronic acid 5 mg IV over 15-30 minutes (single dose)** – preferred for high bone turnover; lasts 1-2 years. Must:
    - Check renal function (eGFR ≥35 mL/min/1.73m²; if lower, avoid or use alternative).
    - Check calcium, phosphate, vitamin D, and correct if deficient.
    - Give with IV fluids (500 mL 0.9% saline) pre- and post-infusion.
    - Monitor for acute phase reaction (fever, myalgia 24-48h post – treat with paracetamol).
  - **Alternative: Risedronate 30 mg PO once daily for 2 months** – used if IV not possible; ensure taken on empty stomach with water; also needs calcium/vitamin D.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Bisphosphonates: hypocalcaemia, severe renal impairment, osteomalacia, pregnancy.
  - NSAIDs: avoid if CKD, GI ulcer risk, heart failure.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Zoledronic acid: avoid if eGFR <35 mL/min/1.73m²; consider oral bisphosphonate (e.g., risedronate) with dose adjustment if eGFR low – [check local policy].
  - No hepatic dose adjustment needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving zoledronic acid without correcting hypocalcaemia or vitamin D deficiency** – can cause severe, symptomatic hypocalcaemia (tetany, arrhythmias). Always check calcium, vitamin D, PTH, and supplement before infusion.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Pathological fracture (especially femoral shaft with “chalkstick” appearance; needs intramedullary nail).
  - Spinal cord / cauda equina compression.
  - High-output cardiac failure (polyostotic, untreated).
  - Acoustic neuroma (rare but from skull base Pageric expansion).
  - Acute immobilisation hypercalcaemia (if fracture and prolonged bed rest – rare in Paget’s).
- **Chronic:**
  - Osteoarthritis of adjacent joints (hip, knee, shoulder) due to mechanical stress.
  - Hearing loss (progressive, mixed type).
  - Dental problems (jaw involvement, denture fitting issues).
  - Osteosarcoma (1% of long-standing polyostotic disease – look for sudden new pain, swelling, rising ALP after a period of normalisation; suspect and image aggressively).
  - Basilar invagination (brainstem compression, obstructive hydrocephalus).
sections.Prognosis:
- With treatment (bisphosphonate): symptoms improve in 80-90%; bone turnover normalises; pain and function improve within months. Bone deformity does not reverse.
- Without treatment: slowly progressive pain and deformity; fracture risk increases; hearing loss may be irreversible; rare but life-threatening complications (osteosarcoma, high-output failure). Life expectancy is normal overall.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always test baseline calcium and vitamin D before starting bisphosphonate** – and supplement if low; it’s a common oversight that leads to hypocalcaemia.
- **Use ALP to monitor treatment response** – a fall of ≥50% by 3-6 months after zoledronic acid is expected; if it rises again, repeat treatment.
- **In any patient >55 years with isolated unexplained raised ALP, consider Paget’s** – but rule out liver disease (ALT, GGT) and bone metastases (check PSA in men, breast exam in women).
- **Refer for audiology if skull involvement** – hearing aids can help; bisphosphonate may slow progression but does not restore loss.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not attribute bone pain solely to osteoarthritis in an elderly patient without checking ALP and X-ray** – you may miss Paget’s and delay treatment, increasing fracture risk.
- **Do not give bisphosphonates without ensuring the patient has taken adequate calcium and vitamin D for at least 2-3 days before** – this avoids acute hypocalcaemia.
- **Do not ignore a new, severe, or night pain in a known Paget’s patient** – could be the first sign of osteosarcoma or pathological fracture; image immediately.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** new severe bone pain, limb deformity, inability to weight bear, or sudden worsening of hearing/balance.
- **Fever, myalgia, flu-like symptoms 24-48h after zoledronic acid** – reassure they are common (acute phase reaction), treat with paracetamol.
- **Any signs of fracture (acute shortening, rotation, swelling) or new neurological symptoms (leg weakness, bladder/bowel dysfunction)** – attend ED.
- **Follow-up:** routine clinic review at 3-6 months after treatment to reassess ALP and symptoms; if ALP normalised and symptoms stable, annual review.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call orthogeriatrics / orthopaedic team urgently if:** pathological fracture suspected (pain, deformity, inability to move limb).
- **Call neurosurgery / spine team if:** back pain with leg weakness, sensory level, urinary retention (cord compression); need MRI within hours.
- **Call medical registrar / ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, or signs of high-output failure (tachycardia, hypotension, oedema, raised JVP).
- **If osteosarcoma suspected (rapidly expanding painful swelling, rising ALP despite treatment)** – urgent referral to sarcoma MDT, discuss with oncology/orthopaedic oncology.

rawMarkdown:
## Paget Disease of Bone

**One-liner:** A chronic metabolic bone disorder causing focal, disorganised bone remodelling; common in elderly UK/Irish population, leading to pain, deformity, fracture, and rarely high-output failure.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Pathological fracture** (especially femoral neck or shaft) – acute, severe pain, inability to weight bear, limb deformity; this is a surgical emergency, orthogeriatric requirement.
- **Spinal cord compression** from pagetic vertebral expansion or fracture – new onset back pain, leg weakness, urinary retention, sensory level; needs urgent MRI and neurosurgery consult.
- **High-output cardiac failure** (rare but lethal) – widespread polyostotic disease with warm, vascular bone; look for tachycardia, wide pulse pressure, signs of heart failure in an elderly patient with known Paget’s.

### Presentation
- Bone pain (deep, aching, worse at night, improved by lying down) – typically pelvis, femur, skull, spine.
- Deformity: bowing of tibia or femur, skull enlargement, “hat size increase”.
- Warmth over affected bone (increased vascularity).
- Hearing loss (bilateral, conductive ± sensorineural due to skull base involvement).
- Neurological: spinal stenosis, cranial nerve palsies (e.g., optic atrophy), basilar invagination.
- Incidental finding on X-ray (asymptomatic) – very common.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Osteoarthritis** – pain worse with activity, joint-line tenderness, no bone deformity or warmth; X-ray shows joint space narrowing, osteophytes, not pagetic trabeculae.
- **Metastatic bone disease (prostate, breast, lung, renal)** – often lytic or blastic lesions, may have history of malignancy; check PSA, alkaline phosphatase may be raised but also raised in Paget’s; bone scan can help but appearance differs.
- **Osteomalacia** – widespread bone pain, muscle weakness, low serum calcium/phosphate, raised ALP; imaging shows Looser zones (pseudofractures), not the coarse trabeculae of Paget’s.
- **Hyperparathyroidism (osteitis fibrosa cystica)** – hypercalcaemia, raised PTH, subperiosteal bone resorption on X-ray; Paget’s has normal calcium and PTH.
- **Fibrous dysplasia** – presents earlier (children/young adults), often monostotic, X-ray shows “ground glass” matrix; not a disease of the elderly.
- **Splitter:** If ALP >500 U/L and X-ray shows thickened, coarsened trabeculae with bone enlargement → Paget’s; if ALP high but calcium high and X-ray shows subperiosteal resorption → hyperparathyroidism.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Measure height (serial loss may indicate vertebral fracture), check pulses and capillary refill in affected limbs; do a basic neurological exam (tone, power, sensation, reflexes, plantar responses).
- **Bloods:**
  - ALP (bone-specific isoenzyme if available) – main marker; very high in active disease (often >500 U/L, can be >2000).
  - Calcium, phosphate – normal.
  - PTH – normal (may be secondarily raised if calcium low – rare).
  - 25-hydroxyvitamin D – check before bisphosphonate therapy.
  - Urea and creatinine (for renal function before IV bisphosphonate).
  - FBC, ESR/CRP (to exclude other causes of raised ALP, e.g., liver disease).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Plain X-ray of symptomatic area – diagnostic features: osteoporosis circumscripta cranii (skull), coarsened trabeculae, bone enlargement, “picture frame” vertebra, “bowed” long bones, fissure fractures.
  - **Second-line:** Technetium-99m bone scan – to assess extent of polyostotic disease (bilateral, asymmetrical uptake). Can be used to guide therapy if localised.
  - MRI if spinal cord compression or suspected pathological fracture.
  - CT if planning orthopaedic surgery.
- **Never-miss test:** Plain X-ray of any painful bone (to rule out fracture) + ALP level – omission may mean treating pain as arthritis while missing incipient pathological fracture.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute fracture or major deformity: immobilise, analgesia, orthogeriatric referral; do not delay surgery.
  - If suspected cord compression: urgent MRI, dexamethasone (10 mg IV stat), consult neurosurgery.
  - If high-output failure: treat heart failure (diuretics, rate control) and urgent Paget’s therapy.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis with X-ray and labs.
  - Assess pain: simple analgesia (paracetamol, NSAIDs if not contraindicated) – but do not delay definitive therapy.
  - Start bisphosphonate therapy (see Drugs & Doses) – NICE recommends treatment for symptomatic disease or to prevent complications (e.g., at risk sites, deformity, deafness).
  - Ensure good vitamin D and calcium intake (oral calcium 500-1000 mg daily + vitamin D 800-2000 IU daily) – crucial before/during bisphosphonate to avoid hypocalcaemia.
  - Physiotherapy review for gait aids, exercises.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Monitor ALP every 6-12 months; aim to normalise or reduce by ≥50%.
  - Repeat bisphosphonate if ALP rises again (usually 1-2 years later).
  - Annual hearing assessment if skull involvement.
  - Orthopaedic follow-up if deformity or joint involvement.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reason):**
  - Fluids: 1 L Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline over 8-12h (if nil otherwise) – no special need unless pre-bisphosphonate loading.
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD if weight >50 kg, renal function ok).
  - Diet/NBM: unless fracture management, allow normal diet.
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly; if high-output failure or acute fracture, 1-2 hourly initially.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - **Zoledronic acid 5 mg IV over 15-30 minutes (single dose)** – preferred for high bone turnover; lasts 1-2 years. Must:
    - Check renal function (eGFR ≥35 mL/min/1.73m²; if lower, avoid or use alternative).
    - Check calcium, phosphate, vitamin D, and correct if deficient.
    - Give with IV fluids (500 mL 0.9% saline) pre- and post-infusion.
    - Monitor for acute phase reaction (fever, myalgia 24-48h post – treat with paracetamol).
  - **Alternative: Risedronate 30 mg PO once daily for 2 months** – used if IV not possible; ensure taken on empty stomach with water; also needs calcium/vitamin D.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Bisphosphonates: hypocalcaemia, severe renal impairment, osteomalacia, pregnancy.
  - NSAIDs: avoid if CKD, GI ulcer risk, heart failure.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Zoledronic acid: avoid if eGFR <35 mL/min/1.73m²; consider oral bisphosphonate (e.g., risedronate) with dose adjustment if eGFR low – [check local policy].
  - No hepatic dose adjustment needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving zoledronic acid without correcting hypocalcaemia or vitamin D deficiency** – can cause severe, symptomatic hypocalcaemia (tetany, arrhythmias). Always check calcium, vitamin D, PTH, and supplement before infusion.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Pathological fracture (especially femoral shaft with “chalkstick” appearance; needs intramedullary nail).
  - Spinal cord / cauda equina compression.
  - High-output cardiac failure (polyostotic, untreated).
  - Acoustic neuroma (rare but from skull base Pageric expansion).
  - Acute immobilisation hypercalcaemia (if fracture and prolonged bed rest – rare in Paget’s).
- **Chronic:**
  - Osteoarthritis of adjacent joints (hip, knee, shoulder) due to mechanical stress.
  - Hearing loss (progressive, mixed type).
  - Dental problems (jaw involvement, denture fitting issues).
  - Osteosarcoma (1% of long-standing polyostotic disease – look for sudden new pain, swelling, rising ALP after a period of normalisation; suspect and image aggressively).
  - Basilar invagination (brainstem compression, obstructive hydrocephalus).

### Prognosis
- With treatment (bisphosphonate): symptoms improve in 80-90%; bone turnover normalises; pain and function improve within months. Bone deformity does not reverse.
- Without treatment: slowly progressive pain and deformity; fracture risk increases; hearing loss may be irreversible; rare but life-threatening complications (osteosarcoma, high-output failure). Life expectancy is normal overall.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always test baseline calcium and vitamin D before starting bisphosphonate** – and supplement if low; it’s a common oversight that leads to hypocalcaemia.
- **Use ALP to monitor treatment response** – a fall of ≥50% by 3-6 months after zoledronic acid is expected; if it rises again, repeat treatment.
- **In any patient >55 years with isolated unexplained raised ALP, consider Paget’s** – but rule out liver disease (ALT, GGT) and bone metastases (check PSA in men, breast exam in women).
- **Refer for audiology if skull involvement** – hearing aids can help; bisphosphonate may slow progression but does not restore loss.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not attribute bone pain solely to osteoarthritis in an elderly patient without checking ALP and X-ray** – you may miss Paget’s and delay treatment, increasing fracture risk.
- **Do not give bisphosphonates without ensuring the patient has taken adequate calcium and vitamin D for at least 2-3 days before** – this avoids acute hypocalcaemia.
- **Do not ignore a new, severe, or night pain in a known Paget’s patient** – could be the first sign of osteosarcoma or pathological fracture; image immediately.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** new severe bone pain, limb deformity, inability to weight bear, or sudden worsening of hearing/balance.
- **Fever, myalgia, flu-like symptoms 24-48h after zoledronic acid** – reassure they are common (acute phase reaction), treat with paracetamol.
- **Any signs of fracture (acute shortening, rotation, swelling) or new neurological symptoms (leg weakness, bladder/bowel dysfunction)** – attend ED.
- **Follow-up:** routine clinic review at 3-6 months after treatment to reassess ALP and symptoms; if ALP normalised and symptoms stable, annual review.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call orthogeriatrics / orthopaedic team urgently if:** pathological fracture suspected (pain, deformity, inability to move limb).
- **Call neurosurgery / spine team if:** back pain with leg weakness, sensory level, urinary retention (cord compression); need MRI within hours.
- **Call medical registrar / ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, or signs of high-output failure (tachycardia, hypotension, oedema, raised JVP).
- **If osteosarcoma suspected (rapidly expanding painful swelling, rising ALP despite treatment)** – urgent referral to sarcoma MDT, discuss with oncology/orthopaedic oncology.
